hidden_gems.name: Cascina delle Rose
hidden_gems.address: Strada Rio Sordo 58, loc. Tre Stelle, 12050 Barbaresco, Italy
hidden_gems.what_locals_love: A 5.5-hectare family estate atop the Tre Stelle cru, farming organically (uncertified), fermenting with native yeasts and keeping sulfites low. The Barbaresco Tre Stelle and Rio Sordo are pure, perfumed and made in tiny quantities that rarely leave Italy.
hidden_gems.tip: Visits are by appointment with the family; ask to taste Tre Stelle beside Rio Sordo to read two adjacent crus side by side.
hidden_gems.description: Five-and-a-half hectares on the Tre Stelle cru of Barbaresco, farmed organically by the Sobrino brothers. Native-yeast, low-sulfite Nebbiolo of rare perfume and delicacy.

verified.address_quoted: Str. Rio Sordo, 58, loc. Tre Stelle, 12050 Barbaresco

hidden_gems.name: Trediberri
hidden_gems.what_locals_love: Father-and-son Federico and Nicola Oberto farm organically at Torriglione, making clean, fragrant La Morra Barolo from Berri and Rocche dell'Annunziata. Prices stay fair while neighbouring crus climb out of reach.
hidden_gems.tip: The Barolo del Comune di La Morra is the entry; book a Torriglione visit to taste it beside the single-cru Rocche dell'Annunziata.
hidden_gems.description: Young La Morra estate founded in 2007 by the Oberto family, farming organically at Torriglione. Elegant, fairly priced Barolo from Berri and Rocche dell'Annunziata.

hidden_gems.name: Brovia
hidden_gems.address: Via del Grosso 5, 12060 Castiglione Falletto, Italy
hidden_gems.what_locals_love: A traditionalist family estate farming organically in every sense without certification, bottling four single crus: Rocche di Castiglione, Villero, Garblet Sue and Brea Ca Mia. Long macerations and large botti, classic and undervalued.
hidden_gems.tip: Quietly one of the best traditional addresses in Castiglione Falletto; ask the family for the Rocche di Castiglione, their most ethereal cru.
hidden_gems.description: Traditionalist Castiglione Falletto estate founded in 1863, farming organically uncertified. Four single-cru Barolos in a classic, long-maceration style at fair prices.

hidden_gems.name: Cavallotto Tenuta Bricco Boschis
hidden_gems.address: Via Alba-Monforte 104, fraz. Bricco Boschis, 12060 Castiglione Falletto, Italy
hidden_gems.what_locals_love: A 25-hectare monopole-like single block around Bricco Boschis, farmed organically since the 1970s. The family releases its Barolo only when ready, often years late, making it one of the great-value traditional houses.
hidden_gems.tip: The Riserva Vignolo and Bricco Boschis San Giuseppe age for decades; cellar releases mean you can still buy mature vintages at the estate.
hidden_gems.description: Castiglione Falletto estate farming a 25-hectare single block at Bricco Boschis organically since the 1970s. Late-released, age-worthy traditional Barolo at honest prices.

verified.address_quoted: Via Alba-Monforte 104, Bricco Boschis, 12060 Castiglione Falletto

hidden_gems.name: Comm. G.B. Burlotto
hidden_gems.what_locals_love: A 19th-century Verduno house whose foot-trodden Barolo Monvigliero has become one of the most sought bottles in the Langhe. They also keep alive Verduno Pelaverga, a peppery local rarity grown almost nowhere else.
hidden_gems.tip: Monvigliero allocations vanish; the Verduno Pelaverga is the affordable, spicy curiosity to take home from the cellar instead.
hidden_gems.description: Historic Verduno estate founded around 1850, famous for foot-trodden Barolo Monvigliero and the rare peppery Verduno Pelaverga grown almost nowhere else.

hidden_gems.name: Ferdinando Principiano
hidden_gems.what_locals_love: One of Piedmont's most respected natural growers, farming organically and biodynamically without certification since 2004. Whole-cluster, low-sulfite Barolo from Ravera and Boscareto that reads more savoury and floral than the modernist norm.
hidden_gems.tip: Visits to the Via Alba cellar are by appointment; the entry Dolcetto Sant'Anna shows the no-sulfite-added natural style at a low price.
hidden_gems.description: Respected Monforte natural grower farming organically and biodynamically (uncertified) since 2004. Whole-cluster, low-sulfite Barolo from Ravera and Boscareto.

hidden_gems.name: Cascina Tavijn
hidden_gems.what_locals_love: Fourth-generation grower Nadia Verrua makes native-yeast, nothing-added natural wines from the Asti hills, championing the rare Ruche and old-vine Grignolino that most of Piedmont overlooks. Five hectares ringed by hazelnut groves.
hidden_gems.tip: Few places take Grignolino and Ruche seriously; Tavijn's are the cult natural versions, sold mostly through tiny importers, so buy at the cellar.
hidden_gems.description: Fourth-generation Scurzolengo estate making native-yeast, nothing-added natural wine. A rare champion of old-vine Grignolino and the obscure Ruche grape.

hidden_gems.name: Crissante Alessandria
hidden_gems.address: Frazione Santa Maria, Borgata Roggeri 44, 12064 La Morra, Italy
hidden_gems.what_locals_love: A small Santa Maria family grower with own-rooted Galina vines going back to 1922. Quietly traditional La Morra Barolo, plus a house-made Barolo Chinato, all at prices the famous La Morra names have left behind.
hidden_gems.tip: Ask for the Galina from the pre-phylloxera own-rooted parcel, and a glass of their Barolo Chinato to finish.
hidden_gems.description: Small La Morra family estate at Santa Maria with own-rooted Galina vines from 1922. Traditional, affordable Barolo and a house-made Barolo Chinato.

verified.address_quoted: Fraz. Santa Maria Borgata Roggeri, 44 12064 - La Morra
